docs.title: pd
docs.text:
Recursive grid-decomposition circuit structures.

A circuit is built over a multi-dimensional grid of variables by recursively cutting the current hypercube along candidate split coordinates. Each cut yields a product of the two sub-hypercubes; a region mixes the products of all its admissible cuts. Leaf hypercubes either factorize into per-variable inputs or delegate to a user-supplied sub-circuit builder.

This file also provides a data-driven variant whose leaf regions are hidden tree-structured sub-circuits.

docs.title: PD(data_shape, num_latents, *, split_intervals=None, split_points=None, max_split_depth=None, structure_type='sum_dominated', input_dist=None, num_cats=256, sum_concentration=1.0, seed=None)
docs.text: Build a recursive grid-decomposition circuit over a hypercube of variables.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: data_shape: Grid shape; the number of variables is the product of the dimensions, indexed in row-major (C) order. num_latents: Number of sum nodes per region. split_intervals: Spacing of candidate split coordinates per dimension (int or per-dim sequence). If neither this nor split_points is given, each dimension is split at its midpoint. split_points: Explicit candidate split coordinates per dimension. max_split_depth: Optional cap on recursion depth; regions reaching it are factorized. structure_type: "sum_dominated" (index-aligned products) or "prod_dominated" (all pairwise products before mixing). input_dist: Leaf distribution; defaults to a categorical over num_cats. sum_concentration: Dirichlet concentration for randomly initialized weights. seed: Optional RNG seed for reproducibility.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The result is smooth and decomposable (valid for likelihood, sampling, and maximum-likelihood training). Because a region mixes several distinct decompositions, it is generally not structurally decomposable, so pairwise transport / expectation queries between two such circuits may not apply.

Source code in sparc/structures/pd.py 
def PD(
    data_shape: Sequence[int],
    num_latents: int,
    *,
    split_intervals: Optional[Union[int, Sequence[int]]] = None,
    split_points: Optional[Sequence[Sequence[int]]] = None,
    max_split_depth: Optional[int] = None,
    structure_type: str = "sum_dominated",
    input_dist: Optional[InputDistribution] = None,
    num_cats: int = 256,
    sum_concentration: float = 1.0,
    seed: Optional[int] = None,
) -> Circuit:
    """Build a recursive grid-decomposition circuit over a hypercube of variables.

    Parameters
    ----------
    data_shape:
        Grid shape; the number of variables is the product of the dimensions,
        indexed in row-major (C) order.
    num_latents:
        Number of sum nodes per region.
    split_intervals:
        Spacing of candidate split coordinates per dimension (int or per-dim
        sequence). If neither this nor ``split_points`` is given, each dimension
        is split at its midpoint.
    split_points:
        Explicit candidate split coordinates per dimension.
    max_split_depth:
        Optional cap on recursion depth; regions reaching it are factorized.
    structure_type:
        ``"sum_dominated"`` (index-aligned products) or ``"prod_dominated"``
        (all pairwise products before mixing).
    input_dist:
        Leaf distribution; defaults to a categorical over ``num_cats``.
    sum_concentration:
        Dirichlet concentration for randomly initialized weights.
    seed:
        Optional RNG seed for reproducibility.

    Notes
    -----
    The result is smooth and decomposable (valid for likelihood, sampling, and
    maximum-likelihood training). Because a region mixes several distinct
    decompositions, it is generally not structurally decomposable, so pairwise
    transport / expectation queries between two such circuits may not apply.
    """
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    dist = resolve_input_distribution(input_dist, num_cats)
    return _PD(
        data_shape,
        num_latents,
        split_intervals=split_intervals,
        split_points=split_points,
        max_split_depth=max_split_depth,
        structure_type=structure_type,
        dist=dist,
        concentration=sum_concentration,
        leaf_builder=None,
    )

docs.title: PDHCLT(data, data_shape, num_latents, *, split_intervals=None, split_points=None, max_split_depth=None, structure_type='sum_dominated', input_dist=None, num_cats=256, num_bins=32, sum_concentration=1.0, seed=None)
docs.text:
Grid-decomposition circuit whose leaf regions are hidden tree sub-circuits.

Identical grid structure to :func:PD, but each leaf hypercube is built as a hidden tree-structured sub-circuit over its variables, estimated from the corresponding columns of data.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
data: 2-D array (n_samples, n_vars); column v corresponds to variable v in the grid's row-major order. data_shape: Grid shape (its product must equal the number of data columns). num_latents: Number of sum nodes per region and per leaf-subtree variable. num_bins: Bins used for mutual-information estimation in the leaf sub-circuits.

See :func:PD for the remaining parameters and the structural-decomposability note.

Source code in sparc/structures/pd.py 
def PDHCLT(
    data: np.ndarray,
    data_shape: Sequence[int],
    num_latents: int,
    *,
    split_intervals: Optional[Union[int, Sequence[int]]] = None,
    split_points: Optional[Sequence[Sequence[int]]] = None,
    max_split_depth: Optional[int] = None,
    structure_type: str = "sum_dominated",
    input_dist: Optional[InputDistribution] = None,
    num_cats: int = 256,
    num_bins: int = 32,
    sum_concentration: float = 1.0,
    seed: Optional[int] = None,
) -> Circuit:
    """Grid-decomposition circuit whose leaf regions are hidden tree sub-circuits.

    Identical grid structure to :func:`PD`, but each leaf hypercube is built as a
    hidden tree-structured sub-circuit over its variables, estimated from the
    corresponding columns of ``data``.

    Parameters
    ----------
    data:
        2-D array ``(n_samples, n_vars)``; column ``v`` corresponds to variable
        ``v`` in the grid's row-major order.
    data_shape:
        Grid shape (its product must equal the number of data columns).
    num_latents:
        Number of sum nodes per region and per leaf-subtree variable.
    num_bins:
        Bins used for mutual-information estimation in the leaf sub-circuits.

    See :func:`PD` for the remaining parameters and the structural-decomposability
    note.
    """
    data = np.asarray(data)
    if data.ndim != 2:
        raise ValueError("data must be 2-D (n_samples, n_vars)")
    n_vars = int(np.prod([int(x) for x in data_shape]))
    if data.shape[1] != n_vars:
        raise ValueError("data columns must equal the product of data_shape")
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)

    dist = resolve_input_distribution(input_dist, num_cats)

    def leaf_builder(factory: _NodeFactory, variables: List[int], k: int) -> Block:
        data_sub = data[:, variables]
        return hclt_block(
            factory,
            data_sub,
            variables,
            k,
            k,
            num_bins=num_bins,
            dist=dist,
            concentration=sum_concentration,
        )

    return _PD(
        data_shape,
        num_latents,
        split_intervals=split_intervals,
        split_points=split_points,
        max_split_depth=max_split_depth,
        structure_type=structure_type,
        dist=dist,
        concentration=sum_concentration,
        leaf_builder=leaf_builder,
    )

docs.title: rat_spn
docs.text:
Randomized, tensorized sum-product circuit structures.

A circuit is built from several independent repetitions. Each repetition recursively partitions the variable scope into num_pieces random sub-scopes down to depth levels (or until a single variable remains), placing a product block at each split and a sum block of num_latents mixtures at each region. The repetitions' top blocks are merged under a single root mixture, yielding a mixture over random circuit shapes.

docs.title: RAT_SPN(num_vars, num_latents, depth, num_repetitions, *, num_pieces=2, input_dist=None, num_cats=256, sum_concentration=1.0, seed=None)
docs.text: Build a randomized tensorized sum-product circuit.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
num_vars: Number of observed variables (indexed 0 .. num_vars - 1). num_latents: Number of sum nodes per region. depth: Maximum recursive partition depth before regions are factorized. num_repetitions: Number of independent random partitions mixed at the root. num_pieces: Branching factor of each partition. input_dist: Leaf distribution; defaults to a categorical over num_cats. sum_concentration: Dirichlet concentration for randomly initialized sum weights. seed: Optional RNG seed for reproducibility.

Source code in sparc/structures/rat_spn.py 
def RAT_SPN(
    num_vars: int,
    num_latents: int,
    depth: int,
    num_repetitions: int,
    *,
    num_pieces: int = 2,
    input_dist: Optional[InputDistribution] = None,
    num_cats: int = 256,
    sum_concentration: float = 1.0,
    seed: Optional[int] = None,
) -> Circuit:
    """Build a randomized tensorized sum-product circuit.

    Parameters
    ----------
    num_vars:
        Number of observed variables (indexed ``0 .. num_vars - 1``).
    num_latents:
        Number of sum nodes per region.
    depth:
        Maximum recursive partition depth before regions are factorized.
    num_repetitions:
        Number of independent random partitions mixed at the root.
    num_pieces:
        Branching factor of each partition.
    input_dist:
        Leaf distribution; defaults to a categorical over ``num_cats``.
    sum_concentration:
        Dirichlet concentration for randomly initialized sum weights.
    seed:
        Optional RNG seed for reproducibility.
    """
    if num_vars < 1:
        raise ValueError("num_vars must be >= 1")
    if num_latents < 1:
        raise ValueError("num_latents must be >= 1")
    if num_pieces < 2:
        raise ValueError("num_pieces must be >= 2")
    if num_repetitions < 1:
        raise ValueError("num_repetitions must be >= 1")
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)

    factory = _NodeFactory()
    dist = resolve_input_distribution(input_dist, num_cats)

    def region(scope: Sequence[int], level: int):
        scope = list(scope)
        if len(scope) == 1:
            leaves = input_block(factory, scope[0], num_latents, dist)
            return summate(factory, [leaves], num_latents, sum_concentration)
        if level >= depth:
            # Factorize the remaining scope into per-variable leaves.
            var_blocks = [
                input_block(factory, v, num_latents, dist) for v in scope
            ]
            prod = product_block(factory, var_blocks)
            return summate(factory, [prod], num_latents, sum_concentration)
        parts = _random_partition(scope, num_pieces)
        if len(parts) == 1:
            # Degenerate split (all items in one piece): factorize instead.
            var_blocks = [
                input_block(factory, v, num_latents, dist) for v in scope
            ]
            prod = product_block(factory, var_blocks)
            return summate(factory, [prod], num_latents, sum_concentration)
        sub_blocks = [region(part, level + 1) for part in parts]
        prod = product_block(factory, sub_blocks)
        return summate(factory, [prod], num_latents, sum_concentration)

    full_scope = list(range(num_vars))
    rep_blocks = [region(full_scope, 0) for _ in range(num_repetitions)]
    root = summate(factory, rep_blocks, 1, sum_concentration)
    return root_circuit(root)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Runnable scripts live in the examples/ directory. Run from the repository root:

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/<script>.py

docs.title: mle
docs.text:
Maximum-likelihood training of a random EmbeddingBuilder circuit by projected gradient ascent.

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/mle.py

docs.title: cw_minimization
docs.text:
Minimize Circuit-Wasserstein distance from a fixed circuit P to a learnable circuit Q using cw_distance_and_grad.

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/cw_minimization.py

docs.title: gcw_optimization
docs.text:
Optimize the GCW cross-term between two circuits (minimize or maximize):

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/gcw_optimization.py --direction min
PYTHONPATH=. python examples/gcw_optimization.py --direction max

docs.title: exp_minimization
docs.text:
Minimize log_exp_query (log expectation) of one circuit under another.

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/exp_minimization.py

docs.title: dro
docs.text:
Distributionally robust optimization saddle-point demo on synthetic circuits.

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/dro.py

docs.title: robustify
docs.text:
DRO-style robustification on pre-trained circuits in examples/example_pcs/.

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/robustify.py

docs.title: structures
docs.text:
Demonstrates all built-in structures and evaluates CW distance between pairs.

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/structures.py

docs.title: bench_gcw
docs.text:
Benchmark GCW forward and gradient passes with optional profiling.

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/bench_gcw.py

docs.title: train_mnist
docs.text:
Train an HCLT circuit on downsampled MNIST (requires matplotlib, torchvision):

PYTHONPATH=. python examples/train_mnist.py --epochs 10

docs.title: Pre-trained circuits
docs.text: The examples/example_pcs/ directory contains JSON circuits for datasets including adult, plants, bbc, and others. Load them with load_learned_pc or Circuit.load.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: SparC ships Cython/C++17 extensions, so a C++ compiler is required.

docs.title: Editable install
docs.text:
pip install -e .

This compiles all extension modules in place. On Linux and macOS you need g++ or clang++; on Windows, MSVC with C++17 support.

docs.title: Optional extras
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[dev]"    # pytest, scipy (solver cross-checks in tests)
pip install -e ".[docs]"   # MkDocs site builder
pip install -e ".[gurobi]" # optional Gurobi extra (not used by core library)

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.9
  • NumPy >= 1.20
  • C++17 compiler

docs.title: Verify the install
docs.text:
from sparc import Circuit, CategoricalInputNode, SumNode, ProductNode

x = CategoricalInputNode(id=0, scope_var=0, probabilities=[0.7, 0.3])
root = SumNode(id=1, children=[x], parameters=[1.0])
Circuit(root).log_likelihood({0: 0})

docs.title: Building documentation locally
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[docs]"
mkdocs serve

Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 to browse the site.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This page walks through constructing a small circuit, evaluating it, and sampling from it.

docs.title: Construct a circuit
docs.text:
A probabilistic circuit is a DAG of sum (mixture), product (factorization), and input (leaf) nodes:

import numpy as np
from sparc import CategoricalInputNode, SumNode, ProductNode, Circuit

# Two categorical variables mixed by a sum over their product
x0 = CategoricalInputNode(id=0, scope_var=0, probabilities=[0.7, 0.3])
x1 = CategoricalInputNode(id=1, scope_var=1, probabilities=[0.5, 0.5])
prod = ProductNode(id=2, children=[x0, x1])
root = SumNode(id=3, children=[prod], parameters=[1.0])

circuit = Circuit(root)

docs.title: Inference
docs.text:
circuit.log_likelihood({0: 0, 1: 1})   # single datapoint
circuit.likelihood({0: 0, 1: 1})

# Vectorized log-likelihood: rows = datapoints, cols = variables
data = np.random.randint(0, 2, size=(1000, 2)).astype(np.int32)
circuit.batched_log_likelihood(data)

docs.title: Sampling
docs.text:
samples = circuit.sample(5, seed=0)
# [{0: 0, 1: 1}, ...]

docs.title: Save and load
docs.text:
circuit.save("model.json")
loaded = Circuit.load("model.json")

See Serialization for the on-disk format and Training for parameter learning.

docs.title: Training
docs.text: SparC optimizes circuit parameters with projected gradient ascent on the mean log-likelihood. Sum-node weights and leaf PMFs live on probability simplices, so each step projects back onto the feasible set.

docs.title: MLETrainer
docs.text:
from sparc.optim import MLETrainer

trainer = MLETrainer(circuit, lr=0.5, method="tangent")
history = trainer.fit(dataset, epochs=100)
  • dataset: iterable of {var: value} dicts (complete assignments).
  • method: "tangent" (project gradient onto simplex tangent, clip, renormalize) or "euclidean" (exact Euclidean projection after the step).

docs.title: Manual gradient steps
docs.text:
For custom objectives (e.g. minimizing a circuit distance), compute a GradBundle from a query and apply projected steps yourself:

from sparc.optim import apply_grads

val, grad = cw_distance_and_grad(p, q)
apply_grads(q, grad, lr=1e-2, ascent=False)  # minimize w.r.t. q

docs.title: API reference
docs.text:
• MLETrainer
  • simplex_step
  • apply_grads
  • mean_log_likelihood_and_grad

See also the MLE example script.

docs.title: Builders
docs.text: The sparc.builders subpackage constructs random circuits for benchmarks, experiments, and baselines.

docs.title: EmbeddingBuilder
docs.text:
Recursively partitions a fixed variable scope with optional node reuse:

from sparc.builders import EmbeddingBuilder

circuit = EmbeddingBuilder(
    num_vars=8,
    num_categories=3,
    sum_arity=2,
    prod_arity=2,
    sum_concentration=1.0,
    sum_reuse_probability=0.0,
    prod_reuse_probability=0.0,
    input_distribution="categorical",
    alpha=1.0,
).build()

Parameters:

  • sum_arity / prod_arity: branching at sum and product nodes.
  • sum_reuse_probability / prod_reuse_probability: probability of reusing a cached subtree for the same scope.
  • input_distribution: "categorical" (Dirichlet PMF) or "binomial".

docs.title: Region graphs
docs.text:
For explicit hierarchical structure, build a region graph first:

from sparc.builders import RandomRegionGraph, RegionEmbeddingBuilder

rg = RandomRegionGraph(
    starting_scope=frozenset(range(8)),
    partitions_per_region=2,
    sub_regions_per_partition=2,
)
region = rg.generate(frozenset(range(8)))

circuit = RegionEmbeddingBuilder(
    region_graph=region,
    num_categories=3,
    block_size=2,
    sum_concentration=1.0,
    input_distribution="categorical",
    alpha=1.0,
).build()

Each region is realized as block_size parallel sum nodes; partitions become product nodes over aligned sub-region blocks.

docs.title: API reference
docs.text:
• EmbeddingBuilder
  • RegionEmbeddingBuilder
  • RandomRegionGraph

docs.title: Extending SparC
docs.text: SparC is designed so new leaf types, ground metrics, and pairwise queries can be added without editing existing query code.

docs.title: New leaf type
docs.text:
1. Subclass InputNode for likelihood and sampling only, or FiniteDiscreteInputNode to also support CW/GCW/expectation queries.
  2. Override the cdef hooks: prob_c, sample_into_c, and (for finite discrete) support_size, pmf_at, scope_var_c.
  3. Register cloning in circuit._clone_node if you use Circuit.clone.
  4. Add serializer support in CircuitSerializer if you need save/load.

No changes are required in eval.pyx or query modules — dispatch goes through the leaf vtable and node_kind tags.

docs.title: New ground metric
docs.text: Subclass GroundMetric and implement pairwise (same support) and cross (two supports) cost matrix fill methods. Pass your metric to CW/GCW/ESD query functions via the metric argument.

docs.title: New pairwise query
docs.text:
1. Subclass CoupleContext in a new Cython module under sparc/queries/.
  2. Implement couple_value for the forward recursion.
  3. Define TapeEntry subclasses with backward methods for reverse-mode gradients.
  4. Export cpdef wrapper functions and add them to sparc/queries/__init__.py.

See the query engine handbook for the shared tape, memo, and product-child matching machinery.

docs.title: New structure
docs.text: Add a constructor under sparc/structures/ using the block algebra in _blocks.py and pluggable InputDistribution specs.

docs.title: Example scripts
docs.text: The examples overview includes optimization loops that combine queries with apply_grads.

docs.title: Queries
docs.text: SparC provides differentiable queries over probabilistic circuits. Pairwise queries require structurally compatible circuits: equal scopes, matching decompositions at sum and product nodes, and compatible leaf cardinalities.

docs.title: Supported queries
docs.text:
Query Math Gradients w.r.t. CW \(W_p^p\) (Circuit-Wasserstein objective) circuit2 only GCW cross-term Gromov-Circuit-Wasserstein cross-term circuit2 only Expectation \(E_Q[P(X)] = \sum_x P(x)\,Q(x)\) both circuits Log expectation \(\log E_Q[P(X)]\) both circuits ESD \(E[d(X,X')^p]\) for two i.i.d. draws single circuit

The CW value is the additive \(W_p^p\) objective; take the \(p\)-th root for the distance itself.

docs.title: Circuit-Wasserstein (CW)
docs.text:
from sparc import cw_distance, cw_distance_and_grad, PNormMetric

val = cw_distance(p, q, metric=PNormMetric(p=2.0, scale=1.0))
val, grad = cw_distance_and_grad(p, q)  # grad w.r.t. q only

Leaf couplings use the northwest-corner plan; sum-sum couplings solve a transport LP with duals for the subgradient.

docs.title: Gromov-Circuit-Wasserstein (GCW)
docs.text:
from sparc import gcw_crossterm, gcw_crossterm_and_grad, gcw_coupling_circuit

cross = gcw_crossterm(c1, c2)
cross, grad = gcw_crossterm_and_grad(c1, c2)

coupling = gcw_coupling_circuit(c1, c2)
coupling.sample(1000)  # joint (x, y) pairs

The coupling circuit lives over vars(c1) plus vars(c2) shifted to a disjoint range. Ancestral sampling draws joint pairs whose marginals match the inputs.

docs.title: Expectation queries
docs.text:
from sparc import exp_query, log_exp_query, exp_query_and_grad

val = exp_query(p, q)
log_val = log_exp_query(p, q)
val, g1, g2 = exp_query_and_grad(p, q)

docs.title: Expected squared distance (ESD)
docs.text:
from sparc import expected_squared_distance, expected_squared_distance_and_grad

val = expected_squared_distance(circuit, metric_p=2.0)
val, grad = expected_squared_distance_and_grad(circuit)

docs.title: Structural compatibility
docs.text:
Queries fail at runtime when:

  • Scopes differ between paired circuits.
  • A sum node in one circuit pairs with a product node in the other.
  • Leaf supports or cardinalities disagree on the same variable.

See test_query_compatibility.py for concrete error cases.

docs.title: Ground metrics
docs.text: Pass a custom GroundMetric or use the default PNormMetric with \(d(i,j) = |i-j|^p / \mathrm{scale}\).

docs.title: API reference
docs.text:
• sparc.queries
  • Query engine handbook

docs.title: Serialization
docs.text: Circuits are saved as UTF-8 JSON in the gcw-circuit-v1 format.

docs.title: Save and load
docs.text:
from sparc import Circuit

circuit.save("model.json")
loaded = Circuit.load("model.json")

Lower-level access via CircuitSerializer:

from sparc.io import CircuitSerializer

json_text = CircuitSerializer.dumps(circuit.root)
root = CircuitSerializer.load("model.json")

Shared subgraphs (DAG reuse) are deduplicated by object identity during serialization.

docs.title: Pre-trained circuits
docs.text:
load_learned_pc loads circuits from a standard directory layout:

{base_dir}/{structure}/{dataset}/{block_size}/
    {structure}_{dataset}_blocksize{block_size}_seed{seed}.json

from sparc.io import load_learned_pc

circuit = load_learned_pc(
    "examples/example_pcs",
    structure="hclt",
    dataset="adult",
    block_size=2,
    seed=0,
)

docs.title: Supported leaf kinds
docs.text:
Kind Fields categorical scope, params (PMF) bernoulli scope, p literal scope, value indicator scope, value, num_cats discrete_logistic scope, mu, s, num_cats sum params, children product children

See the serialization format handbook for the full JSON schema.

docs.title: Structures
docs.text: The sparc.structures subpackage provides ready-made circuit constructors. Each returns a Circuit.

docs.title: Built-in structures
docs.text: Constructor Description HMM Hidden Markov model with categorical emissions GeneralizedHMM HMM with pluggable leaf distribution HCLT Hidden tree from data (MI + MST) PD Recursive grid decomposition PDHCLT PD with HCLT leaf regions RAT_SPN Randomized tensorized sum-product network

docs.title: Example: HCLT from data
docs.text:
import numpy as np
from sparc.structures import HCLT

data = np.random.randint(0, 4, size=(500, 8))
circuit = HCLT(data, num_latents=4, num_bins=16, seed=0)

docs.title: Leaf distributions
docs.text:
Structures accept an optional InputDistribution:

Class Leaf type Categorical Random categorical PMF Bernoulli Binary leaf Indicator Deterministic categorical Literal Deterministic boolean DiscreteLogistic Discretized logistic 
from sparc.structures import GeneralizedHMM, Bernoulli

circuit = GeneralizedHMM(
    seq_length=10,
    num_latents=4,
    input_dist=Bernoulli(p=0.3),
    seed=0,
)

docs.title: Block algebra
docs.text:
Internally, structures compose Block objects (lists of sum nodes) via product and sum operations. This keeps structure code independent of the Cython node layer.

See the architecture handbook for how builders, structures, and the node core connect.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
SparC layers a small Python API over a Cython/C++17 core optimized for CPU inference and differentiable queries.

flowchart TB
    subgraph userLayer [User Layer]
        Circuit[Circuit wrapper]
        Builders[builders/]
        Structures[structures/]
        Optim[optim.MLETrainer]
        IO[io/ serializer]
    end

    subgraph cythonCore [Cython Core]
        Nodes[nodes.pyx]
        Eval[eval.pyx]
        Grad[grad.pyx]
        Graph[_graph.pyx]
    end

    subgraph queries [Pairwise Queries]
        Engine[queries/_engine.pyx]
        CW[cw.pyx]
        GCW[gcw.pyx]
        Exp[expectation.pyx]
        ESD[esd.pyx]
    end

    subgraph solvers [Solvers]
        Transport[transport.pyx]
        Assign[assignment.pyx]
        NW[northwest.pyx]
    end

    Builders --> Nodes
    Structures --> Builders
    Circuit --> Eval
    Circuit --> Grad
    Eval --> Graph
    Grad --> Graph
    CW --> Engine
    GCW --> Engine
    Exp --> Engine
    ESD --> Graph
    CW --> Transport
    CW --> NW
    GCW --> Transport
    GCW --> Assign
    GCW --> NW
    Optim --> Grad

docs.title: Package layout
docs.text: Path Role sparc/circuit.py High-level Circuit wrapper sparc/nodes.pyx Node types and leaf vtable sparc/eval.pyx Likelihood, sampling, CompiledCircuit sparc/grad.pyx GradBundle, MLE gradients sparc/metrics.pyx Pluggable ground metrics sparc/_graph.pyx Flattened CompiledGraph for fast paths sparc/queries/ CW, GCW, expectation, ESD sparc/solvers/ Transport, Hungarian, NW coupling sparc/builders/ Random circuit construction sparc/structures/ HMM, HCLT, PD, RAT-SPN, ... sparc/io/ JSON serialization sparc/optim.py Simplex-projected optimization

docs.title: Node model
docs.text:
• SumNode: mixture over children; parameters on the simplex.
  • ProductNode: product over children with disjoint scopes.
  • InputNode: leaf with prob_c / sample_into_c vtable hooks.
  • FiniteDiscreteInputNode: extends leaves with support_size / pmf_at for Wasserstein and expectation queries.

docs.title: Data flow
docs.text:
1. User builds or loads a circuit (Circuit(root)).
  2. Single-datapoint queries walk the object graph with memoization.
  3. Batched evaluation and query fast paths flatten the DAG into CompiledGraph / CompiledCircuit and run nogil loops.
  4. Gradients accumulate into GradBundle dicts keyed by node.id.
  5. sparc.optim projects gradient steps back onto simplices.

docs.title: Related handbooks
docs.text:
• Query engine
  • Compiled evaluation
  • Solvers

docs.title: Compiled Evaluation
docs.text: Batched log-likelihood and several query fast paths flatten the circuit DAG into a CompiledGraph (sparc._graph) or user-facing CompiledCircuit (sparc.eval.CompiledCircuit).

docs.title: Why flatten?
docs.text: The object-graph evaluator dispatches through Python/Cython method calls per node. For datasets with many rows, flattening to CSR-like arrays allows a single nogil pass over all samples.

docs.title: CompiledCircuit
docs.text:
compiled = circuit.compile()
log_lls = compiled.log_likelihood(data, var_to_col=None)

Requirements:

  • Leaves must be FiniteDiscreteInputNode over a single variable (the leaf family used by OT/expectation queries).
  • Unsupported leaf types set has_fallback=True; the library falls back to the object-graph path automatically.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
The flattened representation stores:

  • kinds: per-node type tag (input / product / sum)
  • child_off, children_flat: CSR child indices
  • sum_logw_flat: log mixture weights for sum nodes
  • leaf_var, leaf_card, leaf_logpmf_flat: leaf metadata and log-PMFs

docs.title: Single-datapoint path
docs.text: likelihood, log_likelihood, and sample try the flat path when possible; otherwise they use memoized recursion on the live node objects.

docs.title: Gradients
docs.text: mean_log_likelihood_and_grad uses the same flat graph when available, accumulating into a GradBundle over the full dataset in one reverse pass.

docs.title: Query Engine
docs.text: Pairwise queries (CW, GCW, expectation) share machinery in sparc.queries._engine.

docs.title: CoupleContext
docs.text:
Each query subclasses CoupleContext and implements:

  • couple_value(P, Q, side_P, side_Q): recursive forward evaluation for a aligned pair of nodes from circuits 1 and 2.
  • Tape entries: TapeEntry subclasses store forward state and implement backward(ctx, g) for reverse-mode accumulation.

docs.title: Memoization and tape
docs.text:
Two keying schemes are used deliberately:

Structure Key Purpose Coupling memo / tape Python object identity of (P, Q) Unique across two circuits Returned gradients node.id Match grads to user-visible nodes

The engine appends tape entries during forward evaluation when recording=True, then replays them in reverse to populate sum_grads* and cat_grads* arrays.

docs.title: Product-child matching
docs.text: When both paired nodes are products over the same scope, children are matched by variable scope (not by pointer identity). Unmatched children raise compatibility errors.

docs.title: Gradient semantics
docs.text:
Query family Gradients returned CW, GCW w.r.t. circuit2 only Expectation, log expectation w.r.t. both circuits ESD w.r.t. the single input circuit

Apply gradients with apply_grads; remember to project onto simplices for probability parameters.

docs.title: Per-query specialization
docs.text:
Module Leaf coupling Sum-sum coupling Product-product cw.pyx NW plan Transport LP + duals Scope-matched product gcw.pyx NW + cross costs Transport + assignment Hungarian matching expectation.pyx PMF product Weighted child recursion Scope-matched product

See Solvers for the underlying OT and assignment routines.

docs.title: Serialization Format (gcw-circuit-v1)
docs.text:
Circuits are stored as UTF-8 JSON with top-level fields:

{
  "format": "gcw-circuit-v1",
  "backend": "numpy",
  "root": 3,
  "nodes": [ ... ]
}

docs.title: Node records
docs.text:
Nodes appear in post-order (children before parents). Each record has:

Field Description id Integer node id (contiguous 0 .. N-1) kind Node type (see below) children List of child node indices type-specific Parameters, scope, etc.

docs.title: Internal nodes
docs.text:
sum

{"id": 3, "kind": "sum", "children": [1, 2], "params": [0.6, 0.4]}

product

{"id": 2, "kind": "product", "children": [0, 1]}

docs.title: Leaf nodes
docs.text:
categorical

{"id": 0, "kind": "categorical", "children": [], "scope": [0], "params": [0.7, 0.3]}

bernoulli

{"id": 0, "kind": "bernoulli", "children": [], "scope": [0], "p": 0.3}

literal

{"id": 0, "kind": "literal", "children": [], "scope": [0], "value": 1}

indicator

{"id": 0, "kind": "indicator", "children": [], "scope": [0], "value": 2, "num_cats": 4}

discrete_logistic

{"id": 0, "kind": "discrete_logistic", "children": [], "scope": [0], "mu": 0.0, "s": 1.0, "num_cats": 8}

docs.title: DAG sharing
docs.text: If two parents reference the same child object in memory, the serializer emits one node record and multiple parent indices pointing to it. On load, each index reconstructs a distinct Python object (no automatic re-sharing).

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Node ids must be contiguous from zero.
  • Children indices must be strictly less than the parent index (post-order).
  • Categorical PMFs must be single-variable and sum to 1.
  • Unknown kinds or unsupported legacy types (e.g. gaussian) raise ValueError.

Implementation: CircuitSerializer.

docs.title: Solvers
docs.text: Wasserstein-type queries reduce inner coupling steps to three built-in solvers under sparc.solvers.

docs.title: Northwest coupling (northwest.pyx)
docs.text: Builds a monotone coupling plan on the integer line for leaf-leaf CW/GCW couplings. Used when both sides are finite discrete leaves with a ground metric cost matrix.

docs.title: Transport (transport.pyx)
docs.text: Solves a minimum-cost flow / transportation LP via a network simplex implementation with dual variables. Sum-sum CW couplings use transport plans; duals supply subgradients w.r.t. marginal weights.

docs.title: Assignment (assignment.pyx)
docs.text: Hungarian algorithm for minimum-cost bipartite matching. GCW uses assignment at product-product nodes to pair children with minimum cross cost.

docs.title: When each solver runs
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    CW[CW query]
    GCW[GCW query]
    CW --> NW1[NW at leaves]
    CW --> TP[Transport at sum-sum]
    GCW --> NW2[NW at leaves]
    GCW --> TP2[Transport at sum-sum]
    GCW --> HUN[Hungarian at prod-prod]

All solvers are pure Cython/C++ with no runtime dependency beyond NumPy arrays for exposing results to Python.

Tests in tests/test_solvers.py cross-check against SciPy when available.
